title: O-Minimality and Rational Numbers
speaker: Gal Binyamini
abstract:
Simonyi 101 and Remote Access
I'll give a brief introduction to o-minimality and how it can be used to prove asymptotic estimates for the number of rational points in definable sets. I'll then show how problems from various areas of mathematics can be reformulated as questions about rational points in suitable definable sets, and what implications one can derive from o-minimality in these cases.

proof_stages.label: Sharp o-minimal structures and complexity filtration
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines an o-minimal structure as a collection of definable subsets of real powers and sharpens it by adding a format-degree filtration compatible with Boolean operations, projection, products, algebraic hypersurfaces, and one-dimensional finiteness bounds.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and calibration of sharpness
proof_stages.summary: From the one-dimensional sharp finiteness axiom one obtains polynomial bounds for connected components in all dimensions, and standard structures are compared to indicate which o-minimal settings are or are not sharp.

proof_stages.label: Rational point counting and the algebraic-transcendental split
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines height-bounded rational points, removes the positive-dimensional semialgebraic part of a definable set, and states the Pila-Wilkie theorem giving an H^epsilon bound on the remaining transcendental part.

proof_stages.label: Sharp derivatives and polylogarithmic point counting
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by Wilkie's conjectural improvement from H^epsilon to powers of log H, a sharp-derivative condition is introduced and a theorem is stated giving polynomial dependence on degree and log H for rational points in sharp o-minimal structures with sharp derivatives.

proof_stages.label: Z-tame reduction principle
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces Z-tame sets, unions of integer fibres of definable families, as a framework for translating questions about non-definable oscillatory objects into integer-point counting problems in definable parameter spaces.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic template and algebraic-obstruction analysis
proof_stages.summary: Countable special sets such as roots of unity, torsion points, and CM points are presented as Z-tame, leading to the Pila-Zannier strategy: lift the desired count to a definable parameter space and use Ax-Schanuel-type results to handle algebraic parts.

proof_stages.label: Exponential-sum application via parameter counting
proof_stages.summary: For Shapiro's conjecture on common zeros of coprime exponential sums, Z-tameness of exponentials turns common-zero counting into integer counting for pairs of definable fibres, with classical Ax-Schanuel controlling the possible algebraic locus.

proof_stages.label: Special-function and harmonic-analysis applications
proof_stages.summary: The method is extended to oscillatory special functions, especially Bessel functions and orthogonal-polynomial asymptotics, yielding sparse counterexample bounds for Fuglede-type orthogonality and related common-zero problems once suitable Ax-Schanuel statements are proved.

examples.name: Semialgebraic sets as the smallest o-minimal structure
examples.note: The speaker notes that closing the real field operations under the axioms gives semialgebraic sets, illustrating the sharp filtration as an extension of algebraic degree.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker identifies semialgebraic sets as what you get by adding polynomial zero sets and closing under the axioms, and states sharpness, but does not compute or verify anything on a particular semialgebraic example.

examples.note: Restricted analytic functions and then the unrestricted exponential function provide standard o-minimal structures, used to show that o-minimality alone need not give sharp degree control.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes globally subanalytic sets via restricted analytic functions and notes R_an,exp is obtained by adding unrestricted exp and is o-minimal, without working through a concrete set or construction beyond the general definition.

examples.engagement_reason: The restricted Pfaffian-type structure is cited as a sharp reduct containing certain restricted functions and coming from triangular differential equations, but no specific Pfaffian example is analyzed.

examples.name: Integer points on y = 2^x
examples.note: The curve y=2^x shows that even in differential-equation-generated structures one cannot generally improve below logarithmic growth, since integer x give integer y.
examples.engagement_reason: The curve y=2^x is used concretely to show sharpness by observing that integer x give integer y and hence yield logarithmically many integer points by height.

examples.name: Graph of sine as a Z-tame set
examples.note: The graph of sin x is not definable in an o-minimal structure because it oscillates, but it is assembled from integer translates of one definable compact piece, making the Z-tame idea concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The graph of sine is checked as Z-tame by restricting sine to one period and gluing the full oscillating graph from integer translates, also noting why it is not o-minimal.

examples.name: Roots of unity parameterized by integer fibres
examples.note: Roots of unity are parameterized by a definable compact exponential graph with integer numerator-denominator parameters, illustrating the Pila-Zannier conversion of special points into integer counting.
examples.engagement_reason: The roots of unity are explicitly parameterized by integer fibres using x=e^{2πia/b} with integer a,b, and the speaker explains why this gives every root while staying definable on a compact domain.

examples.name: Shapiro conjecture for common zeros of exponential sums
examples.note: Finite exponential sums are Z-tame, so the problem of bounding common zeros of coprime exponential polynomials is reduced to counting integer parameter pairs after controlling algebraic parts by Ax-Schanuel.
examples.engagement_reason: For Shapiro’s exponential-sum problem the speaker sets up the sums, states the common-zero conjecture and polylog bound, and outlines the reduction to counting integer parameter pairs whose definable fibres share a root.

examples.name: Bessel zeros and Fuglede orthogonal sets for the disk
examples.note: The Bessel function J_1 is shown to be Z-tame via a Poisson-Jacobi asymptotic formula, enabling sparse-counting results for four-point orthogonal configurations in the disk.
examples.engagement_reason: The Bessel/Fuglede example is developed through the Poisson-Jacobi representation of J_1, the equivalence of orthogonality with Bessel-zero distances, the distance variety V_{4,2}, and a reduction to counting integers in a definable preimage.

passes.reason: Four-point orthogonal sets are the critical forbidden threshold for Fuglede’s disk conjecture, with the disk itself maximally rotationally symmetric.
